sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Latent diffusion models have established a new state-of-the-art in high-resolution visual generation. Integrating Vision Foundation Model priors improves generative efficiency, yet existing latent designs remain largely heuristic. These approaches often struggle to unify semantic discriminability, reconstruction fidelity, and latent compactness. In this paper, we propose Geometric Autoencoder (GAE), a principled framework that systematically addresses these challenges. By analyzing various alignment paradigms, GAE constructs an optimized low-dimensional semantic supervision target from VFMs to provide guidance for the autoencoder. Furthermore, we leverage latent normalization that replaces the restrictive KL-divergence of standard VAEs, enabling a more stable latent manifold specifically optimized for diffusion learning. To ensure robust reconstruction under high-intensity noise, GAE incorporates a dynamic noise sampling mechanism. Empirically, GAE achieves compelling performance on the ImageNet-1K $256 \times 256$ benchmark, reaching a gFID of 1.82 at only 80 epochs and 1.31 at 800 epochs without Classifier-Free Guidance, significantly surpassing existing state-of-the-art methods. Beyond generative quality, GAE establishes a superior equilibrium between compression, semantic depth and robust reconstruction stability. These results validate our design considerations, offering a promising paradigm for latent diffusion modeling. Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/sii-research/GAE.

sections.title: Geometric Autoencoder for Diffusion Models
sections.content: Hangyu Liu1 Jianyong Wang2 Yutao Sun1,2 1Shanghai Innovation Institute 2Tsinghua University

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Latent diffusion models have established new state-of-the-art in high-resolution visual generation. Integrating Vision Foundation Model priors improves generative efficiency, yet existing latent designs remain largely heuristic. These approaches often struggle to unify semantic discriminability, reconstruction fidelity, and latent compactness. In this paper, we propose Geometric Autoencoder (GAE), principled framework that systematically addresses these challenges. By analyzing various alignment paradigms, GAE constructs an optimized low-dimensional semantic supervision target from VFMs to provide guidance for the autoencoder. Furthermore, we leverage latent normalization that replaces the restrictive KL-divergence of standard VAEs, enabling more stable latent manifold specifically optimized for diffusion learning. To ensure robust reconstruction under high-intensity noise, GAE incorporates dynamic noise sampling mechanism. Empirically, GAE achieves compelling performance on the ImageNet-1K 256 256 benchmark, reaching gFID of 1.82 at only 80 epochs and 1.31 at 800 epochs without Classifier-Free Guidance, significantly surpassing existing state-of-the-art methods. Beyond generative quality, GAE establishes superior equilibrium between compression, semantic depth and robust reconstruction stability. These results validate our design considerations, offering promising paradigm for latent diffusion modeling. Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/sii-research/GAE.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Diffusion models [Ho et al., 2020, Song et al., 2020] have rapidly become the main component of visual generation tasks, offering level of quality and flexibility of synthesis that has redefined the production of complex high-resolution media. Compared to direct pixel-level generation [Li and He, 2025, Chen et al., 2025a], latent diffusion [Vahdat et al., 2021, Rombach et al., 2022] that operate within compressed space produced by Variational Autoencoder (VAE) have proven significantly more effective. This paradigm now serves as the technical foundation for many current state-of-the-art generative models [Esser et al., 2024, Seedream et al., 2025, Wan et al., 2025]. Recently, the integration of semantic representations into diffusion models has emerged as key strategy to improve learning efficiency and accelerate convergence. To this end, several parallel research trajectories have emerged to bridge the gap between reconstructive autoencoding and representation learning. First, VAE with semantic supervision [Yao et al., 2025a, Chen et al., 2025b] attempts to enhance the latent space by introducing supervised signals during the Autoencoder training stage. Second, alignment-based methods [Yu et al., 2024, Leng et al., 2025] utilize auxiliary losses to align VAE latents with the feature spaces of Vision Foundation Models (VFMs). Alternatively, another significant direction explores the direct integration of VFMs with reconstruction capability [Zheng et al., 2025, Gao et al., 2025, Bi et al., 2025, Gui et al., 2025], to inherit powerful semantic priors for the generative process. Together, these methodologies demonstrate that unifying perceptual understanding with generative reconstruction is critical frontier for developing more efficient and capable diffusion models. Corresponding author. Preprint. Figure 1: Overview of GAE performance. Left: GAE establishes superior Pareto curve between linear probing accuracy and latent dimension. Right: GAE with 32 latent dimension significantly accelerates convergence and delivers superior generation results at both 80 and 800 training epochs. Despite these advancements, the design of latent spaces for diffusion models remains largely heuristic. This is due to fundamental lack of principled guidance, leaving the complex interplay between various latent properties poorly understood. First, alignment-based methods attempt to bridge the gap between reconstructive and semantic spaces, but the diverse range of alignment strategies frequently results in sub-optimal representations. Second, while lower dimensionality usually benefits diffusion training, VFM-based Autoencoder lacks the flexibility to adjust the latent space. Furthermore, despite addressing both semantic and dimensional requirements, frameworks like VTP [Yao et al., 2025b] still exhibit suboptimal generation quality due to inferior reconstruction stability. These discrepancies highlight that existing heuristic methods fail to analyze the diffusion representation space from fundamental perspective, leading to sub-optimal generative performance. In this paper, we propose Geometric Autoencoder (GAE), principled framework designed to systematically address the critical challenges in latent space design for diffusion models. First, by analyzing various alignment paradigms, we identify that constructing low-dimensional semantic representation from existing VFMs provides the most effective guidance for the latent space of Autoencoder. Second, we leverage latent normalization mechanism to optimize the distribution for diffusion learning, replacing the restrictive KL-divergence typically found in standard VAEs to provide more stable and scalable latent manifold. Finally, by incorporating the dynamic noise sampling mechanism [Sun et al., 2024a], GAE achieves robust reconstruction stability under highintensity noise regimes. Collectively, these components allow GAE to overcome the limitations of prior heuristic designs, establishing more robust and principled foundation for latent diffusion modeling. Empirically, GAE achieves compelling performance in high-fidelity visual generation. On ImageNet [Deng et al., 2009] 256 256 generation, GAE achieves gFID of 1.82 at 80 epochs and further reaches 1.31 at 800 epochs without Classifier-Free Guidance, significantly surpassing existing state-of-the-art methods. Beyond generative performance, our analysis demonstrates that GAE achieves superior pareto frontier between compression and semantic information. Specifically, GAE attains impressive linear probing accuracies of 69.4% and 78.3% at 32 and 64 latent dimensions respectively, while maintaining robust reconstruction stability. We further validate the effectiveness of our framework across various latent dimensions, with ablation studies confirming the critical contribution of each design component. These results substantiate our design principles, offering more principled and effective roadmap for future advancements in latent diffusion modeling.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Vision Foundation Models. The landscape of representation learning has been redefined by Vision Foundation Models (VFMs), which provide robust semantic priors through two primary paradigms. The first category comprises vision-language models such as CLIP [Radford et al., 2021] and SigLIP [Zhai et al., 2023, Tschannen et al., 2025], which align visual features with textual descriptions through large-scale contrastive learning. The second category focuses on visual self-supervised learning, ranging from reconstructive frameworks like MAE [He et al., 2022] to discriminative approaches such as SimCLR [Chen et al., 2020] and DINOv2 [Oquab et al., 2023]. While these models excel at visual understanding, their architectures typically lack inherent data compression, and their representations provide no performance guarantees for generative reconstruction. Consequently, these feature spaces cannot be directly adopted as latent representations for diffusion models without reconciling the gap between semantic discriminability and reconstructive fidelity. Representation Alignment for Generation. The integration of semantic representations into diffusion models has recently emerged as pivotal strategy for enhancing learning efficiency and accelerating convergence. This research landscape is primarily characterized by three converging trajectories that seek to bridge the historical gap between reconstructive autoencoding and representation learning. First, researchers explored the augmentation of VAE through semantic supervision, such as VA-VAE [Yao et al., 2025a], AlignTok [Chen et al., 2025b], which introduces supervised signals during the autoencoder training phase to enforce more discriminative latent space. Second, alignment-based methodologies such as REPA [Yu et al., 2024] and REPA-E [Leng et al., 2025] utilize auxiliary objective functions to align the inner representation of latent diffusion model with the high-dimensional feature spaces of pre-trained Vision Foundation Models. Recently, empowering existing Vision Foundation Models with reconstruction capabilities [Zheng et al., 2025, Gao et al., 2025, Bi et al., 2025, Gui et al., 2025] instead of training dedicated autoencoder from scratch also demonstrates superior performance. Adapting the strong vision encoders allows the diffusion objective to directly inherit robust semantic priors. Beyond the adaptation methods, VTP [Yao et al., 2025b] explores holistic approach by joint-training foundational models with contrastive, self-supervised, and reconstruction objectives. Despite these advancements, the current landscape remains fragmented as most methodologies are primarily heuristic and lack systematic analysis, leaving critical gap in the principled integration of perception and reconstruction for diffusion models.

sections.title: 3 Geometric Autoencoder
sections.content: In this section, we present the GAE framework. We first detail the overall architecture, followed by the formulation of the training objectives. 3.1 Architecture As illustrated in Fig. 2, our framework employs dual-branch design consisting of an encoder, including Encoder Ep, Projector Ap, Decoder Dp, and frozen semantic teacher pipeline utilizing VFM followed by Downsampler Esp. Transformer Autoencoder. We leverage the standard ViT [Dosovitskiy et al., 2020] backbone in both Encoder and Decoder with modern architectural refinements, including RMSNorm [Zhang and Sennrich, 2019] and the SwiGLU [Shazeer, 2020]. ViT-based Autoencoder [Teng et al., 2025, Sun et al., 2024a] achieves significantly higher throughput and better scalability during both training and inference compared with convolution-based neural network. Figure 2: Overview of the GAE architecture. The input image is processed through pixel-level branch (Ep, Ap) and frozen semantic branch (VFM, Esp). Semantic Preservation loss Lsp aligns the latent mean with the features from the decoupled Semantic Teacher. Latent Normalization. Given an input image x, the pixel encoder Ep extracts spatial features, which are subsequently mapped to compact latent space through the projector Ap. To ensure numerical stability and well-distributed latent space [Ke and Xue, 2025], we apply parameter-free regularization via RMSNorm. This operation projects the features onto unit hypersphere, effectively bounding the latent values and preventing training collapse. Dynamic Noise Sampling. Following the σ-VAE [Sun et al., 2024a] formulation, we introduce dynamic sampling mechanism to enhance the robustness of the latent representation. Instead of fixed variance, we sample noise scale σ and perturb the normalized latent mean µ with Gaussian noise ϵ. This stochastic process allows the model to learn continuous manifold under varying noise levels. The latent vector is then fed into the pixel decoder Dp to reconstruct the input ˆx. Formally, the reconstruction process is defined as: µ = RMSNorm(Ap(Ep(x))) = µ + σ ϵ, where ϵ (0, 1), σ (0, Cσ) ˆx = Dp(z) where Cσ is hyperparameter controlling the noise sampling level. 3.2 Objective Image Reconstruction. The reconstruction process is optimized using multi-objective loss function comprising pixel-level L1 loss (Lrec), perceptual loss (Llpips), and adversarial loss (Lgan): Lpixel = λrecLrec + λlpipsLlpips + λganLgan (1) Semantic Preservation. To ensure that the compressed latent space retains high-level semantic integrity and discriminability, we introduce Semantic Preservation loss. Specifically, we align the latent mean µ produced by the pixel branch with the feature representations extracted from the Downsampler Esp. The construction of Esp will be introduced in Sec. 4. The alignment is supervised via MSE loss: Lsp = µ Esp(fvf m(x))2 2 (2) Removal of KL Objective. We remove the Kullback-Leibler divergence penalty typically required in VAE frameworks [Kingma and Welling, 2013]. Traditional VAEs rely on KL divergence to push the latent distribution toward standard Gaussian to facilitate sampling. However, by employing latent normalization, we enforce hard geometric constraint that projects the latent mean µ onto unit hypersphere, ensuring it remains bounded and well-distributed. Coupled with dynamic noise sampling mechanism, this approach enhances reconstruction robustness without the instability of weighted KL term. This design preserves the structural integrity of the latent space, which is more conducive to the subsequent denoising process in diffusion training. Overall Objective. is computed as: Integrating the previously defined components, the total optimization objective Ltotal = λrecLrec + λlpipsLlpips + λganLgan + λspLsp (3) Following standard practices in generative modeling [Rombach et al., 2022, Ke and Xue, 2025], we empirically set the balancing hyperparameters to λrec = 1.0, λlpips = 1.0, and λgan = 0.5. For the semantic preservation term, we set λsp = 1.0 to ensure strong alignment between the latent space and the teachers semantic representations.

sections.title: 4 Latent Alignment for Antoencoder
sections.content: In this section, we systematically investigate the design space of semantic alignment between generative autoencoders and Vision Foundation Models. We argue that the stage at which semantic supervision is introduced significantly impacts both reconstruction fidelity and semantic discriminability. Through series of pilot studies, we identify Latent Alignment, direct supervision at the 4 Figure 3: Illustration of the three latent alignment paradigms: The term Lsp aligns the AE representations with those of the VFM. Pre Alignment aligns high-dimensional encoder features directly; Post Alignment projects AE latents back to high-dimensional space for supervision; Latent Alignment operates within the compressed latent bottleneck via projection of VFM features. compact bottleneck as the most effective paradigm for preserving semantic integrity. To implement this, we further propose parametric downsampler to bridge the dimensionality gap, ensuring that the autoencoder inherits the rich, discriminative priors of the VFM without compromising generative performance. 4.1 Design Spaces for Semantic Alignment Fundamental challenge in integrating generative autoencoders with Vision Foundation Models lies in their inherent dimensionality mismatch. While pixel-level Autoencoder typically compresses images into highly compact latent space (e.g., = 32) to facilitate efficient diffusion training, VFMs often operate in high-dimensional semantic space (e.g., = 1024 for ViT-L). The specific stage of alignment dictates the interplay between semantic density and reconstruction fidelity. We categorize and evaluate three distinct alignment methodologies to systematically explore this design space. Taxonomy of Alignment. We categorize existing semantic alignment methodologies into three distinct paradigms based on the architectural stage at which dimensionality reconciliation occurs: Pre Alignment: This strategy aligns the encoders intermediate high dimensional features before the compressed latent with the VFM prior. Post Alignment: This paradigm projects the compressed latents back into highdimensional space via an additional expansion layer for high dimensional supervision. Latent Alignment (Ours): This paradigm reconciles the dimensionality mismatch by introducing semantic downsampler to project the VFMs high-dimensional outputs into compact space that directly matches the AEs latent dimensions. Pilot Study Setup. We instantiate the framework using ViT-L backbones for the Autoencoder and frozen DINOv2-L/14 as the semantic teacher. For Latent Alignment, we utilize pre-computed SVD projection matrix derived from 64, 000 ImageNet samples to map the 1024-dim VFM features onto the 32-dim manifold. All variants undergo 60-epoch training phase. We evaluate the learned representations across two dimensions: reconstruction fidelity and semantic discriminability via Linear Probing (LP) on the stand-alone latents. Analysis and Findings. As detailed in Tab. 1, our analysis reveals critical bottleneck in information flow. While Pre Alignment achieves slight advantage in reconstruction, it suffers from dramatic loss in semantic discriminability. This suggests that supervising the high-dimensional features prior to the projector does not guarantee that semantic integrity is maintained through the subsequent compression stage. In contrast, Latent Alignment demonstrates remarkable capacity to inherit VFM priors, achieving the highest LP accuracy. This result is particularly significant given the 5 Table 1: Pilot Study of alignment paradigms on ImageNet. Metric rFID LP Autoencoder Teacher Pre 0.40 20.9 Post 0.48 60.8 Latent 0.51 63. SVD 60.9 DinoL 83.7 simplicity of the SVD-based downsampler, which suggests that direct supervision at the bottleneck is the most effective way to anchor the latent space. 4.2 Training Regime of Semantic Teacher While the non-parametric SVD projection provides baseline for Latent Alignment, its tokenwise operation neglects essential spatial correlations, thereby restricting the models ability to construct semantically coherent compressed space. To overcome this, we advocate for parametric downsampling approach, where neural network is optimized to distill high-dimensional VFM priors into compact latent form. By learning non-linear mapping instead of static projection, the model can better reconcile structural integrity with latent compactness, ensuring that the resulting teacher representation is both efficient and discriminative. Arch Table 2: Ablation of Downsampler in Semantic Teacher. Training Objective. We leverage Feature Autoencoder [Gao et al., 2025] for the Semantic Teacher training. The framework consists of frozen VFM backbone fvfm, parametric downsampler Esp, and lightweight 4-layer Llama-style [Touvron et al., 2023] Transformer decoder Dsp. To ensure the compressed latents faithfully preserve the teachers foundational knowledge, we pre-train the encoder-decoder pair using feature-based cosine distillation objective. Specifically, we treat the intermediate patch tokens from the frozen VFM as semantic anchors and train the bottleneck to retain sufficient information for their directional recovery. For an input image x, the training loss Lspt is defined as the cosine distance between the teacher features and the reconstructed counterparts: Lspt = cos(Dsp(Esp(fvfm(x))), fvfm(x)) (4) This ensures the resulting low dimensional latents capture the most discriminative semantic components. After this pre-training phase, Dsp is discarded, and the frozen Esp serves as the finalized downsampler to provide semantic supervision for the autoencoder. Single Attn Attn + Linear Attn + Patch Conv 62.8 63.4 75. LP Downsampler Architecture. To determine the optimal configuration for semantic compression, we evaluate three downsampler architectures: (1) Single Attention following FAE [Gao et al., 2025]; (2) Attention+Linear, which employs global linear projection following the attention module; and (3) Attention+Patch Conv, which utilizes non-overlapping patch convolution to project tokens into the latent space. As shown in Tab. 2, we assess the semantic quality of the resulting embeddings via Linear Probing. The results demonstrate that our Patch Conv design significantly outperforms the baselines, confirming that spatial-aware, end-to-end learned downsampler is essential for constructing discriminative teacher latent space.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: In this section, we provide the implementation details and evaluate the proposed framework on the ImageNet-1K [Deng et al., 2009] benchmark. Experimental Setup for Main Generation (Secs 5.1, 5.3). For our primary generation tasks, we instantiate GAE with ViT-L backbone. We leverage DINOv2-L [Oquab et al., 2023] as the frozen Visual Foundation Models. The Autoencoder is trained for 200 epochs with global batch size of 1024 using the AdamW optimizer. For the generative model, we strictly follow the optimization strategy of LightningDiT-XL [Yao et al., 2025a], utilizing constant learning rate of 2.0 104, batch size of 1024, and an EMA weight of 0.9999. To ensure stability, we apply QK-Norm for the 800-epoch runs, while the 80-epoch benchmarks are conducted without QK-Norm. Comprehensive details are provided in Appendix G. 6 Table 3: Quantitative comparison of class-conditional Image Generation on ImageNet 256256. GAE achieves 1.31 FID without guidance and 1.13 with Classifier-Free Guidance, outperforming all prior methods by large margin. denotes RAE utilizes the AutoGuidance [Karras et al., 2024] instead of standard Classifier-Free Guidance. Method Epochs #Params Generation w/o CFG Generation w/ CFG MaskGIT [Chang et al., 2022] LlamaGen [Sun et al., 2024b] VAR [Tian et al., 2024] MagViT-v2 [Yu et al., 2023] MAR [Li et al., 2024] MaskDiT [Zheng et al., 2023] DiT [Peebles and Xie, 2023] SiT [Ma et al., 2024] FasterDiT [Yao et al., 2024] MDT [Gao et al., 2023a] MDTv2 [Gao et al., 2023b] REPA [Yu et al., 2024] VA-VAE [Yao et al., 2025a] REPA-E [Leng et al., 2025] Aligntok [Chen et al., 2025b] RAE (DiT-XL) [Zheng et al., 2025] RAE (DiTDH-XL) [Zheng et al., 2025] FAE [Gao et al., 2025] GAE gFID IS Pre. Rec. gFID IS Pre. Rec. AutoRegressive 227M 3.1B 2.0B 307M 945M 6.18 9.38 3.65 2.35 Latent Diffusion 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 676M 839M 839M 675M 675M 675M 675M 5.69 9.62 8.61 7.91 6.23 5.90 5.14 2.17 3.46 1.70 2.04 1.87 2.16 1.51 2.08 1.48 1.82 1. 182.1 112.9 200.5 227.8 177.9 121.5 131.7 131.3 143.0 130.2 205.6 159.8 217.3 206.2 209.7 214.8 242.9 207.6 239.8 220.4 254.4 0.80 0.69 0.79 0.74 0.67 0.68 0.67 0.71 0.76 0.77 0.77 0.77 0.76 0.80 0.82 0.79 0.82 0.81 0.82 0. 0.51 0.67 0.62 0.60 0.67 0.67 0.69 0.65 0.62 0.65 0.63 0.66 0.67 0.63 0.59 0.63 0.59 0.63 0.61 0.64 2.18 1.80 1.78 1.55 2.28 2.27 2.06 2.03 1.79 1.58 1.42 2.11 1.35 1.67 1.15 1.37 1.41 1.13 1.70 1.29 1.48 1. 263.3 365.4 319.4 303.7 276.6 278.2 270.3 264.0 283.0 314.7 305.7 252.3 295.3 266.3 304.0 293.6 309.4 262.6 243.8 268.0 265.2 294.9 0.81 0.83 0.81 0.80 0.83 0.82 0.81 0.81 0.79 0.80 0.81 0.79 0.80 0.79 0.79 0.80 0.78 0.82 0.80 0.80 0. 0.58 0.57 0.62 0.61 0.57 0.59 0.60 0.61 0.65 0.65 0.58 0.65 0.63 0.66 0.65 0.63 0.67 0.61 0.64 0.62 0.67 555 300 350 1080 800 1600 1400 1400 400 1300 1080 800 64 800 80 800 800 800 80 800 80 800 80 Ablation Study Configuration. Unless otherwise specified, our ablation experiments adopt the ViT-L backbone for the GAE, with default hyperparameters set to λsp = 1.0, = 32, and Cσ = 0.1. These Autoencoders undergo 100-epoch training phase, adhering to the identical learning rate schedule as the 200-epoch primary configuration. For generative assessment, we report results using LightningDiT-XL backbone trained for 80-epoch without QK-Norm. Comprehensive details are provided in Appendix H. Evaluation Metrics. To provide comprehensive assessment, we evaluate the proposed alignment paradigm across three primary dimensions: Semantic Discriminability: We assess the semantic density of the learned latent space via Linear Probing accuracy. Following the protocol in Appendix I, we train linear classifier on the frozen latents of ImageNet to evaluate how effectively the VFMs discriminative priors are preserved after compression. Reconstruction Fidelity: To ensure the autoencoder maintains high-quality image recovery, we report standard pixel-level and perceptual metrics, including PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS, and rFID to measure the statistical distance between the original and reconstructed validation sets, capturing structural and textural consistency. Generation Quality: During generation stage, we evaluate the performance of Latent Diffusion with gFID, Inception Score, Precision and Recall to assess image quality, diversity, fidelity and sample coverage. 5.1 System-level Comparison In this section, we evaluate the class-conditional image generation performance of GAE on the ImageNet benchmark. As summarized in Tab. 3, GAE establishes new SOTA results across multiple generative metrics while maintaining an extremely compact 32-dimensional latent space. Complementary analyses regarding reconstruction performance are provided in Appendix C. Generative Performance. When trained for 800 epochs, our framework attains gFID of 1.31 without CFG and 1.13 with CFG. These results consistently surpass strong baselines such as FAE, 7 Figure 4: Decoder stability against latent noise injection. We evaluate rFID by adding varying levels of Gaussian noise to the latent representations before decoding. The results demonstrate that models trained with higher σ exhibit superior tolerance to latent distribution shifts, ensuring stable performance during generative sampling. which yields 1.48 and 1.29 respectively, and RAE, which achieves 1.51 and 1.13. Notably, while GAE matches the 1.13 gFID of RAE under guided sampling, RAE benefits from the more sophisticated AutoGuidance [Karras et al., 2024] protocol and larger model size. In contrast, our framework achieves comparable performance using only the standard CFG. Training Efficiency and Convergence. One of the most striking advantages of GAE is its remarkable training efficiency. Specifically, at only 80 epochs, GAE achieves gFID of 1.82, which already surpasses the performance of VA-VAE trained for full 800 epochs. This rapid convergence validates our core hypothesis that semantically aligned latent space simplifies the learning objective for the subsequent diffusion model. Semantic-Reconstruction Trade-off. Despite its focus on semantic alignment, GAE does not sacrifice visual fidelity. As illustrated in the Pareto Frontier (Fig. 1, left), GAE achieves high LP accuracy of 69.4%, which is substantially higher than the 43.1% achieved by VA-VAE at the same latent dimension. 5.2 Reconstruction Stability Dynamic Noise Sampling. As illustrated in Fig. 4, our analysis reveals fundamental trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and generative performance governed by the noise strength Cσ. While minimizing Cσ inherently yields superior rFID by reducing the perturbation of the latent manifold, it may fail to provide robust reconstruction performance as the noise level increases. Specifically, for the = 32 configuration, although Cσ = 0.1 maintains slight lead in rFID during early training, this gap diminishes as the model scales, whereas Cσ = 0.2 provides the necessary robustness to stabilize subsequent diffusion learning. In the = 64 case, we observe that generative performance effectively plateaus between Cσ = 0.2 and Cσ = 0.3. Consequently, we identify Cσ = 0.2 for both = 32 and = 64 as the balanced configurations that harmonize semantic density with high-fidelity recovery, consistent with the experiment results in σ-VAE [Sun et al., 2024a]. Analysis on Other Variants. We further extend our analysis to other autoencoder variants in Fig. 4. RAE demonstrates exceptional reconstruction resilience, where its rFID remains remarkably stable even at high perturbation level of σ = 0.5, which directly contributes to its superior gFID. In 8 Table 4: Impact of standard deviation Cσ and latent dimension on ImageNet-1K. gFID is reported without CFG. Autoencoders are trained on ImageNet-1K for 100 epochs. SSIM rFID PSNR LPIPS gFID Dim Cσ 0.05 0.1 0.2 0.1 0.2 0.3 = = 64 0.37 0.45 0.57 0.31 0.43 0.49 27.78 27.37 26.99 29.60 28.92 28.30 0.101 0.107 0. 0.076 0.086 0.094 0.784 0.774 0.759 0.845 0.827 0.811 2.58 2.36 2.32 3.15 3.12 3.11 Table 5: Quantitative comparison in the 64-dim latent space on ImageNet 256256. GAE consistently outperforms existing 64-dim baselines in semantic alignment, reconstruction quality, and generative convergence speed. Method Dim LP Reconstruction gFID (w/o CFG) rFID PSNR LPIPS SSIM 80 Ep 160 Ep 800 Ep VTP-L FAE GAE 64 64 73.9 78.3 0.36 0.66 0.38 26.83 29.09 0.126 0.081 0.750 0.834 2.81 1.96 2.02 1.53 1.29 contrast, VTP exhibits different behavior. While it achieves high semantic discriminability due to its training regime, its reconstruction performance is significantly more sensitive to latent perturbations. The sharp degradation in rFID hinders its generative performance, leading to sub-optimal gFID results. 5.3 Scalability to Higher Dimension In this section, we investigate the scalability of the GAE framework by extending the latent space to 64 dimensions. We are using our GAE variant against two prominent 64-dimensional baselines, VTP-L and the 64-dim version of FAE. The results are summarized in Tab. 5. Semantic and Reconstruction. As shown in Tab. 5, GAE demonstrates superior performance even as the latent dimensionality increases. In terms of semantic discriminability, GAE achieves the highest LP accuracy of 78.3%, notably outperforming VTP-L (73.9%). Regarding reconstruction fidelity, GAE maintains substantial lead over FAE, with an rFID of 0.382 compared to 0.660. Generation Performance. The generative advantages of GAE are also evident in the highdimensional setting. Without the use of CFG, GAE achieves gFID of 1.96 at only 80 training epochs, far surpassing VTP-Ls 2.81. When training progresses to 160 epochs, GAEs gFID further improves to 1.53, which is considerably better than the 2.02 achieved by FAE at the same stage. These results confirm that our semantic alignment mechanism remains highly effective at larger scales, providing superior foundation for diffusion-based image synthesis. 5.4 Ablation Studies 5.4.1 Semantic Supervision Our investigation into the sensitivity of the framework to λsp reveals critical trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and latent discriminability, as detailed in Tab. 6. While λsp = 0.5 yields superior reconstruction metrics, increasing the weight to λsp = 1.0 provides more discriminative latent space and higher Linear Probing accuracy with comparable generation quality. This suggests that the marginal loss in reconstruction fidelity is effectively compensated for by the enhanced semantic priors, especially considering that pixel-level quality can be further recovered with extended training duration. However, increasing the weight further to λsp = 2.0 leads to diminishing returns. The excessive focus on semantic alignment severely compromises reconstruction capacity, creating bottleneck that results in suboptimal gFID of 2.45. Consequently, we identify λsp = 1.0 as the optimal balance that maximizes semantic richness without inducing catastrophic pixel-level degradation. 9 Table 6: Ablation of Semantic loss weight λsp on ImageNet-1K. gFID is reported without CFG. Autoencoders are trained on ImageNet-1K for 100 epochs using consistent 32-dimensional latent bottleneck. λsp 0.0 0.5 1.0 2.0 rFID PSNR LPIPS SSIM LP gFID 0.76 0.40 0.45 0.59 27.16 28.11 27.37 26.50 0.157 0.095 0.107 0.122 0.77 0.796 0.774 0. 5.74 63.5 69.2 71.4 12.55 2.35 2.36 2.45 Table 8: Scalability analysis of the Autoencoder backbone. gFID is reported without CFG. Autoencoders are trained on ImageNet-1K for 100 epochs using consistent 32-dimensional latent bottleneck. Type Dim Layers Heads PSNR LPIPS SSIM gFID rFID LP ViT-L ViT-B ViT-S 1024 768 384 24 12 16 12 6 69.2 62.5 0.45 0.41 1.55 27.37 26.70 23.85 0.107 0.122 0.212 0.774 0.754 0. 2.36 2.43 5.4.2 The Model Size of Autoencoder To investigate the impact of the Autoencoders parameter, we systematically evaluate three Vision Transformer configurations, including ViT-S, ViT-B, and ViT-L. The quantitative results are summarized in Tab. 8. Our analysis reveals that reconstruction fidelity is strongly correlated with the models representative capacity. While the ViT-L backbone achieves superior performance across all reconstruction metrics, the lightweight ViT-S variant exhibits significant degradation. This scaling also extends to semantic preservation, where ViT-L achieves 69.2% Linear Probing accuracy, significantly closing the gap toward the 75.6% performance bound. Notably, despite slightly higher rFID compared to ViT-B, ViT-L yields superior gFID of 2.36, reinforcing the notion that enhanced semantic discriminability provides more effective optimization landscape for latent diffusion. 5.4.3 Latent Normalization Table 7: Comparison between vanilla VAE and AE with Latent Normalization on ImageNet. We investigate the impact of normalization strategies by comparing vanilla VAE against the Autoencoder with Latent Normalization. Both models utilize ViT-L architecture without sp loss and were trained for 100 epochs. The VAE is trained with KL weight of 0.1. As shown in Tab. 7, while both methods effectively constrain the latent space, the Normalization configuration proves significantly more conducive to diffusion model learning. KL constraint Latent Norm Method rFID 0.977 0.764 gFID 16.72 12.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we introduced Geometric Autoencoder (GAE), principled framework that addresses the fundamental limitations of heuristic latent space design in diffusion models. By systematically analyzing the interplay between semantic alignment, latent distribution, and reconstruction stability, we have moved toward more grounded architectural foundation. Our approach successfully bridges the gap between high-level perceptual understanding and low-level generative fidelity, ensuring that the latent manifold is both informationally rich and computationally efficient. GAE demonstrates strong and efficient performance. On ImageNet 256 256, our model without Classifier Free Guidance achieves state of the art gFID of 1.31 with 800 training epochs and 1.82 with only 80 epochs. Additionally, GAE establishes superior equilibrium between compression, semantic depth and robust reconstruction stability. We believe GAE offers promising roadmap for improving latent diffusion training.

sections.title: A Qualitative Results
sections.content: Figure 5: Qualitative results. Samples are generated by GAE (d = 32) after 800 epochs of training. Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) scale of = 3.3 is utilized to enhance visual fidelity.

sections.title: B Extended Generative Performance Across Epochs
sections.content: In this section, Tab. 9 provides detailed breakdown of the generative performance (gFID, without CFG) for GAE with various latent dimensions on the ImageNet 256 256 benchmark across various training stages. Table 9: Detailed gFID scores without Classifier-Free Guidance for GAE with different latent dimension at different training epochs on ImageNet 256 256. Epochs 80 = 32 = 64 1.82 1. 160 1.51 1.53 320 1.37 1.39 480 1.33 1. 640 1.31 1.31 800 1.31 1.

sections.title: C Reconstruction Quality
sections.content: In this section, we provide quantitative comparison of the reconstruction performance between our proposed GAE and various baseline models. As shown in Tab. 10, our model significantly outperforms traditional alignment-heavy frameworks such as RAE and FAE in terms of reconstruction fidelity. Specifically, at 64-dimensional latent space, our method achieves PSNR of 29.085 and an SSIM of 0.834, whereas RAE only yields 18.86 PSNR and 0.62 SSIM.

sections.title: D Effect of Semantic Teacher
sections.content: We compare two representative architectures: MAE-L, which is optimized for pixel-level reconstruction, and DINOv2-L, which is designed for discriminative representation learning. As shown in Tab. 11, while MAE-L achieves superior reconstruction metrics (rFID 0.355, PSNR 28.712), its limited semantic discriminability (29.8% LP) leads to suboptimal gFID of 4.20. In contrast, DINOv2-L provides significantly stronger semantic priors (69.2% LP), which, despite slight decrease in rFID, results in vastly superior gFID of 2.36.

sections.title: E Ablation on CFG Parameters
sections.content: In this section, we investigate the impact of various Classifier-Free Guidance parameters on the generative performance of GAE with 32 dimension. Specifically, we examine the CFG weight (w), 1 Table 10: Quantitative comparison of reconstruction quality on ImageNet-1K. sections.title: F Algorithm of Patch Convolution
sections.content: As detailed in Algorithm 1, our patch-wise module is implemented through two-step procedure designed to optimize computational efficiency. Specifically, we first perform patch-wise convolution that projects each local spatial block directly into high-dimensional feature vector. Subsequently, the resulting feature map is re-partitioned and flattened into the target latent patch format.

sections.title: G Detailed Configuration
sections.content: GAE Training Details. We instantiate our GAE framework with ViT-L backbone utilizing downsampling factor of = 16. Semantic supervision is provided by trained downsampler based on the DINOv2-L architecture. For both 32-dimensional and 64-dimensional latent spaces, we set the 2 Algorithm 1 Patch Convolution with Window Reshaping Require: Feature map RBHW C, window size w. Ensure: Latent map RBHW d. 1: {Phase 1: Local Window Partitioning} 2: Fwin WindowPartition(F, w) {Shape: [B HW 3: Flatten(Fwin) {Flatten each window to [B HW 4: {Phase 2: Patch-wise Joint Projection} 5: XWproj, where Wproj R(w2C)(w2d) 6: {Phase 3: Spatial Structure Reconstruction} 7: Zwin Reshape(Y, [B HW 8: WindowReverse(Zwin, w) {Restore to [B, H, W, d]} 9: return w2 , w2, d]) w2 , w2, C]} w2 , w2 C]} Figure 6: Comparison of downsampler architectures. Left: The FAE downsampler. Right: Our downsampler with patch convolution. semantic preservation loss weight λsp = 1.0 and the latent standard deviation Cσ = 0.2. The GAE is trained for 200 epochs with global batch size of 1024. Diffusion Model Optimization. For the generative process, we strictly adhere to the optimization strategy of LightningDiT-XL/1 [Yao et al., 2025a]. We utilize constant learning rate of 2.0 104, global batch size of 1024, and an EMA weight of 0.9999. To maintain training stability, QK-Norm is applied for the full 800 epoch training runs, whereas the 80 epoch benchmarks are conducted without QK-Norm to match the short-term evaluation protocol. Furthermore, we incorporate an RAE-style [Zheng et al., 2025] time-shift mechanism to optimize the trajectory of the noise schedule during training. Sampling and Evaluation Protocol. Following the sampling protocol established in FAE [Gao et al., 2025], we employ an SDE-based sampler [Song et al., 2020] for evaluations without CFG, and an ODE-based sampler [Chen et al., 2018] for evaluations involving CFG. All generative results are synthesized using 250 sampling steps. Consistent with RAE [Zheng et al., 2025], we perform class-uniform sampling during evaluation, ensuring an equal number of samples are generated for each category to provide balanced assessment of model performance. For the = 32 configuration, hyperparameters are specifically tailored to the training duration: at 800 epochs, we adopt timeshift of 0.4, CFG interval of 0.3, and CFG weight of 3.3. In contrast, for the 80-epoch checkpoint, these parameters are adjusted to 0.4, 0.25, and 2.5, respectively, to optimize for early-stage convergence. For the = 64 variant, timeshift of 0.5 is consistently employed across our evaluations. To ensure statistical reliability, both gFID and rFID metrics are evaluated using 50,000 images for all experiments. 3 Table 13: Detailed architectural and optimization configurations for main generation. Ep and Dp denote the pixel encoder and decoder, while Ap represents the linear projector. The semantic encoder sp integrates DINOv2-L backbone with downsampler, and Dsp serves as an auxiliary decoder for semantic training. Category Field Architecture Optimization Input dim. Output dim. Hidden dim. sections.title: H Detailed Configuration of Ablation Study
sections.content: Unless specified otherwise in individual experiments, the architectural and optimization settings for our ablation studies follow the general configurations described in Sec. G. GAE Training Details. For all ablation variants, we instantiate GAE with ViT-L backbone and default latent dimension of = 32. To accelerate the experimental cycle, GAE is trained for reduced duration of 100 epochs. Following our noise schedule analysis, the latent standard deviation is set to Cσ = 0.1 for these studies. Diffusion Model Optimization. The diffusion models in the ablation section are trained for 80 epochs, which we found sufficient to reflect relative performance gains. Consistent with our shortterm training protocol, QK-Norm is disabled for these runs. All other optimization hyperparameters remain identical to the primary setup defined in Sec. G. Sampling and Evaluation Protocol. Our sampling procedure for ablation follows the protocol established in VAVAE [Yao et al., 2025a]. We primarily report generative performance without classifier-free guidance (CFG) using an ODE-based sampler with 250 steps. Furthermore, class labels are sampled randomly following the VAVAE evaluation standard. For these ablation experiments, the timeshift parameter is fixed at 0.7. To ensure statistical reliability, both gFID and rFID metrics are evaluated using 50,000 images for all experiments.

sections.title: I Linear Probing Evaluation Protocol
sections.content: To quantitatively evaluate the semantic discriminability of the learned latent space, we report ImageNet-1K linear probing Top-1 accuracy. Unlike prior works that rely on DINO-style Linear Probing [Gao et al., 2025, Yao et al., 2025b] by concatenating pooled latents with the original DINO CLS tokens, we assess the learned latents as stand-alone representations to strictly measure their inherent semantic quality. Specifically, we conduct LP tasks using two distinct configurations based on the feature dimensionality in the main paper. We also provide the full set of LP results in Tab. 14: Flatten: For compressed latents (e.g., = 32 or 64), we map the spatial tensor Rd1616 directly to the 1,000 ImageNet classes. This captures both spatial and semantic information within the bottleneck. GAP: For high-dimensional, uncompressed representations (e.g., 1, 024), we apply Global Average Pooling to collapse the spatial dimensions into vector R1024 prior to classification. 4 This prevents the excessive parameter count associated with flattening high-dimensional tensors, which otherwise hinders linear optimization. Optimization Details. The linear probes are trained for 100 epochs using global batch size of 2, 048. We utilize Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer with constant learning rate of 0.008. No weight decay or complex learning rate scheduling is applied during this phase. Table 14: Comprehensive LP results.
